session.tool_result:
1→--- 2→title: GitHub Actions Label Filtering Reference 3→last_audited: "2026-02-16 14:20 PT" 4→audit_result: clean 5→read_when: 6→ - debugging why label-based CI gating isn't working 7→ - implementing label-based workflow conditions 8→ - confused about .*.name syntax vs array filtering 9→tripwires: 10→ - action: GitHub Actions cannot interpolate Python constants 11→ warning: label strings must be hardcoded in YAML 12→ - action: using this pattern 13→ warning: 14→ Always use negation (!contains) for safe defaults on push events without 15→ PR context 16→--- 17→ 18→# GitHub Actions Label Filtering Reference 19→ 20→## Why Label Filtering Is Non-Obvious 21→ 22→GitHub Actions label checks are counterintuitive because: 23→ 24→1. **The syntax hides array operations** — `.*.name` is property extraction, not dot notation 25→2. **Event context matters** — `pull_request.labels` is available only on PR events, not push events 26→3. **Negation determines safe defaults** — `!contains()` ensures push events run CI, `contains()` would skip them 27→ 28→This document explains the WHY behind the pattern, not the syntax itself. 29→ 30→## The Safe Default Problem
... [110 lines omitted] ...

Here's the result of running `cat -n` on a snippet of the edited file: 1→--- 2→title: GitHub Actions Label Filtering Reference 3→last_audited: "2026-02-16 14:20 PT" 4→audit_result: edited 5→read_when: 6→ - debugging why label-based CI gating isn't working 7→ - implementing label-based workflow conditions 8→ - confused about .*.name syntax vs array filtering 9→tripwires: 10→ - action: GitHub Actions cannot interpolate Python constants 11→ warning: label strings must be hardcoded in YAML 12→ - action: using label-based gating 13→ warning: 14→ Always use negation (!contains) for safe defaults on push events without 15→ PR context 16→--- 17→ 18→# GitHub Actions Label Filtering Reference 19→ 20→## Why Label Filtering Is Non-Obvious 21→ 22→GitHub Actions label checks are counterintuitive because: 23→ 24→1. **The syntax hides array operations** — `.*.name` is property extraction, not dot notation 25→2. **Event context matters** — `pull_request.labels` is available only on PR events, not push events 26→3. **Negation determines safe defaults** — `!contains()` ensures push events run CI, `contains()` would skip them 27→ 28→This document explains the WHY behind the pattern, not the syntax itself. 29→
... [71 lines omitted] ...
session.assistant: Now update `github-actions-label-queries.md`:
session.tool_result:
1→--- 2→title: GitHub Actions Label Queries 3→read_when: 4→ - "checking PR labels in GitHub Actions workflows" 5→ - "working with push event workflows" 6→ - "implementing CI gating based on PR labels" 7→tripwires: 8→ - score: 6 9→ action: "Label checks in push event workflows" 10→ warning: "Job-level label access via github.event.pull_request.labels is ONLY available in pull_request events, NOT push events. For push events, you must use step-level GitHub API queries with gh cli or REST API." 11→ context: "The asymmetry exists because push events don't have github.event.pull_request in their context. Defense-in-depth: Keep job-level checks for pull_request events (fast path), add step-level API queries for push events (required path)." 12→last_audited: "2026-02-16 14:20 PT" 13→audit_result: clean 14→--- 15→ 16→# GitHub Actions Label Queries 17→ 18→## The Asymmetry Problem 19→ 20→GitHub Actions workflows face a fundamental asymmetry in how PR labels are accessed: 21→ 22→**pull_request events**: The `github.event.pull_request` context is populated, so labels are available via `github.event.pull_request.labels.*.name` at job evaluation time (before any steps run). 23→ 24→**push events**: The `github.event.pull_request` context does **not exist**. There is no PR context at job evaluation time, even if the push is to a branch with an open PR. 25→ 26→This asymmetry is architectural, not a bug. Push events are branch operations; GitHub Actions doesn't implicitly resolve "what PR is this branch associated with?" until you ask via the API. 27→ 28→## Why This Matters for CI Gating 29→ 30→Any workflow that wants to make decisions based on PR labels must handle both event types correctly:
... [91 lines omitted] ...

session.assistant: Now update `label-rename-checklist.md`:
session.tool_result:
1→--- 2→title: CI Label Rename Checklist 3→read_when: 4→ - renaming a GitHub label used in CI automation 5→ - updating label references across the codebase 6→ - debugging why CI label checks aren't working after a rename 7→tripwires: 8→ - action: "Renaming a GitHub label used in CI automation" 9→ warning: "Labels are referenced in multiple places: (1) Job-level if: conditions in all workflow files, (2) Step name descriptions and comments, (3) Documentation examples showing the label check. Missing any location will cause CI behavior to diverge from intent. Use the CI Label Rename Checklist to ensure comprehensive updates." 10→ score: 6 11→last_audited: "2026-02-16 14:20 PT" 12→audit_result: clean 13→--- 14→ 15→# CI Label Rename Checklist 16→ 17→## Why Label Renames Are Dangerous 18→ 19→GitHub Actions label checks use **string literals in YAML that cannot be validated statically**. Unlike code references that fail at compile time, label mismatches fail silently: 20→ 21→- Boolean expressions evaluate to `false` when label names don't match 22→- No errors are thrown at workflow parse time or runtime 23→- PRs with the label run CI when they should skip (or vice versa) 24→- No obvious indicators point to the mismatch 25→ 26→The problem: **labels connect Python constants, YAML workflow conditions, and prose documentation across three different file formats with zero static validation**. 27→ 28→## The Coordination Challenge 29→ 30→<!-- Source: src/erk/cli/constants.py, PLAN_REVIEW_LABEL -->
... [94 lines omitted] ...
